rationale_for_baseline_tier: Severe unremitting epigastric pain with profuse vomiting, inability to tolerate oral fluids, and clinical signs of early hypovolaemia (weakness, dry mucous membranes, tachycardia and relative hypotension on clinician measurement) require Emergency Department assessment within hours for IV fluid resuscitation, analgesia and confirmatory bloods (lipase, renal function). The source note arranged immediate ED transport via the patient's sibling — self/relative transport is appropriate at this stage, so the correct baseline is T4 (ED, hours), NOT T5. T5 (call 000 ambulance) is reserved for deterioration en route — dizziness/confusion/collapse signalling worsening shock, or pain suddenly spreading across the whole abdomen suggesting perforation.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Continued inability to keep any fluids down with worsening weakness, dizziness or markedly reduced urine output
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Proceed to the Emergency Department now (today, within the next hour) — do not wait for a morning clinic
escalation_edges.rationale: Ongoing fluid loss without replacement drives dehydration and acute kidney injury; only IV resuscitation in hospital corrects it.
escalation_edges.communication_template: You can't keep fluids down and you're getting weaker — you need to go to the Emergency Department now for a fluid drip and blood tests. Have your sibling drive you straight there and don't eat or drink anything on the way.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Feeling dizzy, faint, confused, or collapsing on the way to hospital (signs of worsening shock)
escalation_edges.rationale: Dizziness, confusion or collapse signals decompensating hypovolaemic shock needing an ambulance and pre-hospital resuscitation, not a continued self-drive.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If on the way you feel dizzy or faint, get confused, or you feel like you might pass out — pull over and call 000 for an ambulance right away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Pain suddenly spreading across the whole abdomen, or the belly becoming rigid/board-like
escalation_edges.rationale: Sudden generalisation of pain with rigidity suggests a perforated viscus or peritonitis — a surgical emergency requiring an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain suddenly spreads across your whole tummy or your belly goes hard and rigid, call 000 straight away — that can be a sign of a more serious problem.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever with shaking chills, or the whites of the eyes turning yellow (jaundice)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to the Emergency Department urgently if not already there
escalation_edges.rationale: High swinging fever with rigors or new jaundice raises cholangitis or infected necrosis — needs urgent hospital assessment and antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you spike a high fever with shivering, or your eyes or skin start to look yellow, you need to be seen in the Emergency Department urgently.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives with a supportive sibling who is present but not equipped to manage severe vomiting and dehydration at home
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: The sibling can provide transport to the ED, which supports a T4 self-transport plan; but home management is not viable, so the escalation must be firm and time-bound rather than 'see how you go'.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Early hypovolaemia with acute kidney injury risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Avoid NSAID analgesia advice and avoid oral rehydration; the priority is IV fluids in hospital. Lower the threshold to 000 if any faintness or confusion develops.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to go to the Emergency Department today, within the next hour, for a fluid drip, pain relief and blood tests. Have your sibling drive you straight there. Do not eat or drink anything on the way — not even water. Sit up and lean forward if it eases the pain, and keep a bowl or bag with you for vomiting. Tell the triage nurse straight away about the severe pain going through to your back and the repeated vomiting.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Go directly to the nearest Emergency Department (sibling to drive); call 000 for an ambulance if you deteriorate on the way
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: The Emergency Department is open 24 hours — do not wait for a morning GP clinic. If you cannot safely get there or you are deteriorating, call 000.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Bring a note of when the pain started, the recent heavy drinking, how many times you have vomited, and the paracetamol you took. The hospital will send a discharge summary to your GP for follow-up and alcohol support.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as home-manageable or a routine/next-day GP problem — e.g. advising oral rehydration and rest.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Progressive dehydration, acute kidney injury and hypovolaemic shock; a missed perforation or severe/necrotising pancreatitis deteriorating without hospital care.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Same-day urgent-care review without ensuring ED-level IV resuscitation and bloods — delay to IV fluids and confirmatory bloods while the patient continues to lose volume.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Defaulting to a 000 ambulance when the patient is stable enough for prompt relative-driven ED transport — minimal clinical harm, some avoidable ambulance resource use, clearly preferable to under-triage given under-triage is weighted 3x.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The diagnosis cannot be confirmed remotely, and dangerous mimics (perforated ulcer, atypical inferior MI) cannot be excluded by chat.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate to urgent ED assessment where bloods, ECG and imaging can confirm the diagnosis and exclude mimics; keep nil-by-mouth; safety-net firmly with a 000 threshold.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Anchoring on pancreatitis, reassuring the patient it can be managed at home, or excluding a cardiac/perforation cause without any hospital workup.
